claims.claim: Exact finite tie-aware Best-of-N laws predict selected utility for finite graph-physics candidate pools.
Controlled graph-physics high-N selection can expose selected-tail failures.
Repair components and combined repair improve selected-tail behavior in the synthetic stress suite.
A pilot-label adaptive gate improves high-N selection in the controlled synthetic stress suite.
claims.caveat: Pilot-label synthetic evidence only; not an online deployment guarantee.

claims.claim: A CPU NumPy learned-lite calibrator improves held-out rank alignment in synthetic graph conditions.
claims.caveat: Lightweight synthetic calibration evidence only; not a large learned-simulator claim.

claims.claim: The evidence spans multiple graph families, hidden-failure modes, and stress levels inside a CPU-local synthetic benchmark.
The method is validated on real robot systems.
evidence: No real-robot experiment artifact is present.

claims.claim: The method establishes broad external benchmark superiority or state-of-the-art physics-model performance.
evidence: No broad external benchmark suite is present.

claims.claim: Increasing N universally improves selected real utility.
evidence: The repository studies selected-tail risk, not a universal monotonic-improvement claim.

claims.claim: The adaptive gate proves deployment safety beyond the controlled synthetic setting.
evidence: The adaptive gate is explicitly pilot-label and synthetic.
